title: What is the Mission of the Church, Part 6: Social Justice in Exposition
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: What is the Mission of the Church
source_files: What is the Mission of the Church 6 - Social Justice in Exposition - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: Introduction: The church's mission — making disciples of every nation
The controversy over social justice and mission
The distinction between the church's mission and social responsibility
How discipleship encompasses the whole of Christian life
Conclusion: Clarity on the mission preserves the church's faithfulness
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro concludes the mission series in part 6, addressing the controversial topic of social justice in relation to the church's mission. He expounds the Great Commission to show that making disciples of all nations is the church's primary calling, not social or political transformation.
transcript:
By now you know that the mission of the church is to make disciples of every nation by introducing them to Christ and teaching them to observe all that Christ has commanded. That seems to be very clear from the scripture — not something that should be debated. Yet for the last fifteen to twenty years, the church, especially here in the United States, has not been satisfied with that mission. The idea of making disciples for the Lord Jesus Christ who are going to be forever with him seemed to have been an idea that is not super attractive to the American church. She has largely decided that she can use her time better by relieving human suffering in this life. There is a general change from the focus on the life to come to this life.

Somebody did a study that looked at what the church has been singing for the last hundred years. For the fifty years before that, something like 93 percent of the hymns sung in church had to do with heaven and the life to come and eternal life and the coming of Jesus Christ. Now they said less than 2 percent of the songs sung in church have to do with the life to come, and 98 percent have to do with life here. You see this change of emphasis to the here and now. The idea of living as sojourners and pilgrims in this world has changed, and the church has decided this is our home — we're not just passing through, as the hymns used to say.

So social justice issues have become a big emphasis for the church: social inequality, racial reconciliation, humanitarian efforts — these have become the focal point. It was because of this emphasis on social justice issues, because it has become such a big deal in the American evangelical church, that we decided to cement our understanding of why the church is here. We did this by reading together, as a church starting back in October, Kevin DeYoung and Greg Gilbert's book *What Is the Mission of the Church? Making Sense of Social Justice, Shalom, and the Great Commission*. Our book groups just finished chapter 6 this month, in May. The title of the chapter is "Making Sense of Social Justice in Exposition."

This lesson is supposed to cover that chapter, but we will bleed into the next chapter as well because it is a difficult chapter to teach as a standalone. When you read the book you can just go ahead and read the next chapter — that is no issue — but as far as the lesson, we are going to bleed into chapter 7. Then, Lord willing, in the last Sunday of June, Andrew will bring it all together by covering chapters 7 and 8. I am not going to cover the last couple of chapters of the book in the book study, but I am confident you can read them on your own.

## The Twelve Passages on Social Justice

In this chapter the authors examine twelve separate passages that speak to social justice and the poor — twelve passages that in their studies have been used by evangelicals to justify a change of focus on the mission of the church. They say that for 2,000 years the church has gotten it wrong, and these passages in their new, enlightened way of reading them should guide how we do the ministry of the church and should focus us on the here and now more than on the life to come. So these passages are often used to justify the emphasis on the social justice movement. Here are the twelve:

- Leviticus 19 — the original "love your neighbor as yourself" passage
- Leviticus 25 — the year of Jubilee, probably something that never went beyond the theoretical concept even though it was prescribed to the people of God
- Isaiah 1 — confronting the sins of God's people from a social perspective
- Isaiah 58 — a social justice passage, even though central to Isaiah 58 is the urgency of observing the Sabbath as a spiritual day of worship
- Jeremiah 22 — where Jeremiah speaks of doing justice and righteousness
- Amos 5 — the phrase "let justice roll down like water" was also found in the very famous speech delivered by Martin Luther King Jr. (I am sure Martin Luther also preached on Amos 5, but probably with a different emphasis)
- Micah 6:8 — that very familiar passage: "What has God required of you but to do justly, to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your God?"
- Matthew 25 — the parable of the sheep and the goats
- Luke 10 — the Good Samaritan
- Luke 16 — the rich man and Lazarus
- 2 Corinthians 8 and 9 — where Paul is trying to raise funds for the poor church in Jerusalem
- James 1, 2, and 5 — which talks about faith without works being dead

These twelve passages are analyzed in chapter 6 of our book, some in more detail, some in less. For our lesson today we will consider two of them, and I trust you can read the others on your own, because these two passages seem to be the anchors of most discussion on social justice issues. They are Matthew 25 and Luke 10. We are going to try to cover those two and then address the other ten via Q&A if we have time.

## Matthew 25:31–46 — The Sheep and the Goats

If you have a Bible, please turn to Matthew 25. We are going to read verses 31 through 46. I really encourage you to have a Bible that you use — a Bible that you read, that you know where things are, so that you can picture in your head roughly where a passage sits on the page. It makes your study easier.

Matthew 25, starting at verse 31:

> *"When the Son of Man comes in His glory, and all the holy angels with Him, then He will sit on the throne of His glory. All the nations will be gathered before Him, and He will separate them one from another, as a shepherd divides his sheep from the goats. And He will set the sheep on His right hand, but the goats on the left. Then the King will say to those on His right hand, 'Come, you blessed of My Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world: for I was hungry and you gave Me food; I was thirsty and you gave Me drink; I was a stranger and you took Me in; I was naked and you clothed Me; I was sick and you visited Me; I was in prison and you came to Me.' Then the righteous will answer Him, saying, 'Lord, when did we see You hungry and feed You, or thirsty and give You drink? When did we see You a stranger and take You in, or naked and clothe You? Or when did we see You sick, or in prison, and come to You?' And the King will answer and say to them, 'Assuredly, I say to you, inasmuch as you did it to one of the least of these My brethren, you did it to Me.' Then He will also say to those on the left hand, 'Depart from Me, you cursed, into the everlasting fire prepared for the devil and his angels: for I was hungry and you gave Me no food; I was thirsty and you gave Me no drink; I was a stranger and you did not take Me in, naked and you did not clothe Me, sick and in prison and you did not visit Me.' Then they also will answer Him, saying, 'Lord, when did we see You hungry or thirsty or a stranger or naked or sick or in prison, and did not minister to You?' Then He will answer them, saying, 'Assuredly, I say to you, inasmuch as you did not do it to one of the least of these, you did not do it to Me.' And these will go away into everlasting punishment, but the righteous into eternal life."* (Matthew 25:31–46)

For me it is impossible to read this passage without hearing Keith Green's voice. He has — not quite a song, but a recitation of the passage set to music, very animated. If you are watching on YouTube you can see the energy in his performance as he bangs on the piano. Maybe it is a Sabbath activity: later today, Google "Keith Green and the parable of the sheep and the goats."

Matthew 25 has become a favorite passage of progressive and younger Christians. Few biblical phrases have gotten as much traction as "the least of these." If you Google that, you will come up with all kinds of ministries now labeled "the least of these" that have been created to relieve what is perceived as social injustice. The implications — whether they be increased government spending, increased concern for social justice, or just a general shame over not doing enough — are usually thought to be obvious from this text. But in its popular use, there is almost no careful examination of what Jesus is actually saying. If you stop to actually read the passage and see what Jesus is teaching, you will come up with a very different understanding of what "the least of these" means.

Now the issue is not just with the popular use of the phrase. Even brilliant men have succumbed to this idea. There is a very well-known professor — I believe he is at the University of Virginia — whose name is James Davison Hunter. He wrote a book that was somewhat influential in evangelicalism called *To Change the World*. Hunter argues at one point that Christ makes our treatment of strangers a measure of righteousness. He then quotes Matthew 25 and concludes: "To welcome the stranger — those outside of the community of faith — is to welcome Christ. Believer or non-believer, attractive or unattractive, admirable or disreputable, the stranger is marked by the image of God." He says that is what this passage teaches.

Now it is certainly true that we are all made in God's image. It is also true on other grounds — though not in this passage — that dealing kindly with strangers, even those outside of the church, is a good thing. Look at Galatians 6:10. But it is difficult to conclude that this is Jesus' point in Matthew 25 — that here Jesus teaches us that the church's concern should be to relieve social injustices.

### Who Are "the Least of These"?

That is really the crux of the matter. I am going to summarize what I believe the passage teaches and then try to prove it.

The "least of these" refers to other Christians in need — in particular, itinerant Christian teachers who depend on the hospitality of the family of faith. In our times, these are the people who are being persecuted and need help because they are proclaiming the gospel of Jesus Christ. These are the people who are discipling the nations by introducing Christ to them and teaching them to observe whatever Christ has commanded. These are the people who are proclaiming the gospel where they are and who may be suffering persecution or who simply need the church to come alongside and encourage them and provide for them.

Let me show this from the passage itself.

How many times is that expression used in the passage? Twice. In verse 40 and in verse 45. In verse 45, Jesus uses the phrase "the least of these," but in verse 40 he uses the more exact phrase "the least of these My brethren." The two phrases, as you look at the narrative, refer to the same group. So the more complete phrase of verse 40 should be used to explain the shorter phrase in verse 45. As we harmonize the scriptures — comparing scripture with scripture, what we call the analogy of faith — the fuller account gives us the fuller picture.

So the fuller phrase tells us who the least of these are: they are His *brethren*. And the reference to "my brethren" there in verse 40 cannot be a reference to all suffering humanity, because of how the Bible uses the word "brother." In the New Testament, "brother" is not used for all humanity. The word always refers to either a physical blood brother or to the spiritual family of God. Those are the only two ways the word is used in the New Testament, and you can easily confirm that by just a simple search of the words "brother," "brothers," and "brethren."

So if that is the case, when Jesus says "the least of my brethren," he is either asking us to take care of James and Judas and Joseph — his blood brothers — or he must be telling us to take care of our spiritual brothers. He must be insisting that whatever we do for our fellow Christians in need, we do for him. That seems to be what he is teaching.

This interpretation is confirmed when we look at the last time in Matthew before chapter 25 where Jesus talks about brethren. The best way to define a word in the Bible is to look at the context in which it is used, but also to look at other places where the same author uses it. So here the genre is the Gospels. Then expand to see how it is used in the whole New Testament and the whole Bible, and that gives you a good idea of how the word is used.

In English, think of the word "get." What does it mean? The answer has to be: it depends, because the context determines the meaning. Is it followed by a preposition? "Get up." Is that the same as "get down"? Both use the word "get." Context determines meaning in English, and the same is true in the Bible because the Bible uses language just as we use language.

So looking at the word "brother" in chapter 25, it is important for us to see how Matthew has used it before. If you go back two chapters to Matthew 23 — the very last time that Jesus himself uses the word "brother" before chapter 25 — look at verse 1. He addresses the multitudes and his disciples. In verse 8 he calls them brothers. But in verses 9 and 10 he defines who the brothers are:

> *"Do not call anyone on earth your father; for One is your Father, He who is in heaven. And do not be called teachers; for One is your Teacher, the Christ."* (Matthew 23:9–10)

So in verse 8, when he calls them brothers, he defines them in verses 9 and 10 as those who have one Father who is in heaven and one Teacher who is Christ. Jesus does not call everyone everywhere his brothers. A particular group are his brothers: those who belong to him and do his will.

Remember the incident in Mark 3 where Mary and his blood relatives come to him and are sitting outside, worried that he has gone out of his mind? They say, "Jesus, your mother is out there, and your brothers." And what does he say?

> *"Who are My mother and My brothers? ... Here are My mother and My brothers! For whoever does the will of God is My brother and My sister and mother."* (Mark 3:33–35)

You see that Jesus himself does not look at the word "brother" as meaning everyone in all of humanity, but a specific group of people.

Likewise, it makes more sense to think that Jesus is comparing service to fellow believers with service to him, rather than imagining Jesus to be saying, "You should see my image in the faces of the poor" — which is actually a pretty common expression today: "See the face of Christ in the face of the poor." Jesus does not teach that. What he teaches is that we are to see his face in the face of the brethren, the Christian, because Christ is in us, the hope of glory.

Now, granted, Jesus was a man of sorrows, so we can appropriately identify with him in suffering. He was a suffering Savior and he understands what suffering is — and people who are suffering can hold on to that as an encouragement. But in the rest of the New Testament, it is the body of Christ that represents Christ on earth, not the poor. We do not find out who Christ is by looking at the poor. We find out who Christ is by looking at his bride. She reflects his image to the world. Christ in us is the promise of the gospel for those who believe, not for those living in a certain economic condition. You are not the image of Christ simply by being financially poor. "Blessed are the poor in spirit" — that is the poverty that matters.

Matthew 25 equates caring for Jesus' spiritual family with caring for Jesus. Remember when Paul was going to Damascus to arrest and hurt Christians and Jesus confronts him on the road? Jesus says to him, "Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?" Paul could have said, "Sir, I was a child when you were around. I never persecuted you. I never even witnessed people persecuting you. How can you say that I am persecuting you?" But what did Christ mean? Was Paul persecuting all of humanity in general? No. He was persecuting the church of Jesus Christ. Those are the brethren who are in Christ.

So Matthew 25 does not offer the generic message "care for the poor and you are caring for Christ." That is not the message of chapter 25.

### The Greek Word *Mikros*

Let me go a little deeper into the Greek. The word "least" is a superlative. What is a superlative? The most extreme version of the word. You have "good," the comparative "better," and the superlative "best." You have "great, greater, greatest." So "least" is a superlative of "small."

You know the word *microscope* — a scope that lets you see small things. The Greek word *mikros* means small or little. And in the Gospels, Jesus consistently calls his disciples his "little ones" (*mikroi*). The most enlightening passage is Matthew 10, where he calls his disciples his little ones when he sends them out to proclaim the gospel to the whole nation of Israel. Look at verse 42:

> *"And whoever gives one of these little ones only a cup of cold water in the name of a disciple, assuredly, I say to you, he shall by no means lose his reward."* (Matthew 10:42)

Who are those little ones? Look at the context. In verses 5 through 15, Jesus sends out his disciples to do an itinerant ministry, to go through all Judea and Galilee proclaiming the gospel of Jesus Christ. In the face of persecution in a hostile world, Jesus wants to encourage his followers to care for the traveling ministers no matter the cost. You can see that in verses 16 through 39 of chapter 10. The "little ones" are the disciples — that is how he uses the word *mikros*.

Then when you get to chapter 25, the next time he uses the word *mikros* in the superlative, "the least of these," it makes sense to think he is still talking about his disciples.

## Applications of Matthew 25

Let us talk about some applications before we jump to Luke 10.

Matthew 25 is about social justice in the sense that it is about caring for the needy. But the needy in view are fellow Christians — especially those dependent on our hospitality and generosity for their ministry. "The least of these" is not a blanket statement about the church's responsibility to meet the needs of all the poor, though we do not want to be indifferent to hurting people. Nor should the phrase be used as a general cover for anything and everything we want to promote under the banner of social justice.

What then is our responsibility? Well, Jesus says that if we are too embarrassed, too lazy, or too cowardly to support our fellow Christians who depend on our assistance and who are suffering for the sake of the gospel, we will go to hell. Not my interpretation — look at verse 46: "And these will go away into everlasting punishment, but the righteous into eternal life." Who are "these" who go away to punishment? Those who did not do the things for the least of these my brethren. If that is us, we are going to have hell to face.

Not that helping Christians who are in need because of the gospel saves anyone, but this is one of those fruits of righteousness that signals that we have a genuine faith in Jesus Christ. If the fruit is not there, we have no reason to think that we have the faith.

Secondly, we must care about and care for those who are hungry, thirsty, and homeless because of the gospel. In verse 35, Jesus says, "For I was hungry and you gave Me food; I was thirsty and you gave Me drink; I was a stranger and you took Me in." How do we do that? This includes prayer — we often undervalue prayer. This includes giving money. We are the richest church in the world. We are the richest church in history. And this also includes going to suffer with them, not shrinking back. Unless we are willing to give our children to die for Christ, we are not really serious about the mission of the church. It would perhaps be easier for me to give myself to die for Christ than to give one of my children. But unless we are willing to do that, we really do not understand the importance of the least of these my brethren who are suffering for the sake of the gospel.

Thirdly, we must care about and care for those who are attacked, beaten, and in prison for the sake of the gospel. That is what Jesus says in verse 36. Notice in verse 44 that the goats did not even know that there was such a group of people. They were not even aware that people were suffering for the sake of Christ. We may have a general idea that something is going on somewhere — but who? Who are they? We need to be aware of who they are so we can be involved in their suffering. First Corinthians 12 speaks of the local church as the body of Christ, but the local church is a representation of the visible church as a whole. And if part of the body is hurting, the whole body suffers and we need to suffer with them. That is the social justice that Jesus Christ is looking for in Matthew 25.

**A question from Andrew:** Do you have any comments on using the phrase "social justice"?

I prefer not using it in a favorable way, just because of how it is understood by most people. And because everything is recorded, people can take things out of context. Our culture uses the phrase in a particular way. People say, "Oh, we need to rescue this word or that word" — sure, but we have to be very careful, because does the Bible talk about social justice? I think so. But biblical justice is not equality of outcomes. Biblical justice is equity in the application of the law — the law is applied the same way to the poor and the rich. Not that everybody is going to have the same. So you can talk about social justice in that sense. But if I say "social justice" in a sentence, that is not what most people are going to understand. So if I only have a second, I prefer not to use it. If we are going to have a full conversation, then I can qualify it appropriately.

**A question from a student:** You said the vision of the church is to make disciples. And you said Christians ought to be doing things for the poor. Would it be fair to say that if the Christians in a church body are not caring for the poor, then the church is failing in its mission to make disciples?

Maybe. It is something to look at, because a component of being a disciple of Christ is loving people — primarily the people of God. And that is where the principle of moral proximity will help us navigate. Any other questions?

## Luke 10 — The Good Samaritan

Let us turn to Luke 10. We have about seven minutes for this. That is the parable of the Good Samaritan. Look at verse 25. We have an interaction with a lawyer. He is testing Jesus — not trying to really find the truth, but trying to catch Jesus on his words. And Jesus answers him in a way that actually puts the lawyer in the corner. The lawyer himself quotes Deuteronomy 6 and Leviticus 19, the two passages that are there. This is important: these passages were given specifically to the people of God, not to people in general, but to the covenant community of God, telling them how they should behave toward one another.

The parable that follows in verse 30 is an illustration of those two passages. Now, Luke often focuses on the underprivileged of society and shows how the gospel reaches even to the outcast. You have the prostitute, you have the Samaritan, and so on. The Samaritan in Luke's writings is more often than not the picture of the unlikely convert — someone you would not expect to come to Christ — and yet this man is portrayed as a picture of a Christian.

Think about the priest and the Levite, who walked by. They were raised in the covenant community but acted like outsiders. And the one who came from the outside acted faithfully. Notice also that the man who was beaten up was likely a Jew. Why? The road goes from Jerusalem to Jericho — in Judea, where Jews lived. Also, Jesus goes out of his way to identify the helper as a Samaritan, but he does not say anything specific about the man who was beaten. So more than likely, this man is a Jew, a member of the covenant community.

So the parable of the Good Samaritan is primarily about helping the brother who is right there in front of you. Here, priest — here, Levite — you walked right past him. You saw the brother lying there. You grew up in the church, you knew all about the Word of God, you had been trained — and you saw the need of the brother and you walked away. Here is the unlikely convert to Christ who sees the need of the brother and immediately goes to help. Which one is fulfilling the law of God? The lawyer, the Pharisee, the scribe — who grew up in the church and knows all about the Bible — or the man who was an unlikely convert to Christ and saw the need of the brother and helped him? Jesus says: the Samaritan fulfilled the law. And that is a big slap in the face of the lawyer, who was an expert not in civil law but in the law of God and the Torah.

**A question:** So you are saying this Samaritan was a Christian?

I am saying he was a believer in the God that the lawyer should have been a believer in. So I am asking: was this Samaritan more faithful than you, lawyer, who was trying to catch me in my words?

### The Principle of Moral Proximity

This principle is really what the parable is about. There are so many people needing help all over the world — we cannot possibly help them all. So how do we pick the people we should help? Or are there people we *must* help?

There are two components to answer this question. First, we define who our neighbor is in terms of proximity — literal distance or affinity. And second, we need to define who our neighbor is in relationship to Christ. This helps us distinguish the *must* from the *should*. There is a difference between those two words.

The principle is straightforward but often overlooked: the closer the moral proximity to someone in need, the greater the moral obligation to help. Moral proximity does not only refer to geography, though that can be part of it. It refers to how connected we are to someone by virtue of familiarity, kinship, time, and place. The principle helps us differentiate obligation from wisdom. I *must* help someone who is right next to me. I *should* help someone far away whom I do not know, if I can. Do you see the difference? The Bible teaches that throughout, which is something Andrew will address at the end of June.

**A question:** You emphasized the *must* — but is there a danger that by focusing on the must, people get comfortable and neglect the *should*?

That is a good observation, and probably true. But I think the tendency actually goes the other way. It is easier to fight human trafficking than to proclaim Christ to your sister who does not want to listen to the gospel. It is easier to dig wells in Africa than to talk to your dad who thinks he is sufficient in himself and does not need Christ. So I think the social justice movement is actually something people gravitate toward *because* it is easier — you can actually do it, and it is not as hard as making disciples of Jesus Christ. So yes, we all feel that pull. We might not be involved with the things we should do. But I think it tends to go the other way — away from the gospel, away from the mission that God has given us, making the *should* the *must*, rather than the other way around.

I see hands — I am three minutes over, so send all remaining questions to Andrew. He has every answer. If you go to andrew.hoy at olympiabp.net — no joke — then he can try to address it when he teaches at the end of June. But do it right away; do not wait until the morning of the lesson.
